items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comedic effect is mock-heroic/high-brow phrasing («терзают смутные сомнения») applied to petty suspicion, plus the elliptical parallelism «У X — ..., у Y — ...» meaning each has LOST/been deprived of the item (not simply possesses it). The actual translation keeps the suspicion idea but downgrades the elevated register to casual (“nagging feeling”) and introduces a factual error by using present possession (“has”), which blunts the joke and slightly confuses the logic of the callback (they’re missing those items).

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.

Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is repetitive, banal Soviet-politeness clichés colliding with royal context; the key is the obsessive re-introduction of “Tsar” as if it were a job title/name tag. The translation preserves repetition and awkward politeness well. It slightly shifts from self-introduction (“I’m the Tsar”) to direct address (“Hello, Tsar”), which weakens the specific gag of Bunsha introducing himself as “the Tsar,” but the overall comic effect remains strong and readable.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; preserves the sudden authoritarian, pompous demand that is funny coming from Bunsha.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Meaning and elevated/archaic melancholy are preserved. “Leave me be” is slightly more modern/colloquial than the reference’s more formal cadence, but still works and keeps the contrast with the mundane setting.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: The line is about being walled in/immured; the reference keeps the passive victimhood and the demons as agents. “Bricked up” is idiomatic in English with strong unintended slang connotations and also drops the sense of “they walled me in” (who did it). The second clause keeps “demons” but the first exclamation reads like a modern catchphrase rather than archaic panic, harming comedic intent.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Sense is correct and the religious framing is retained. However, the reference’s “Behold the power…” better matches the mock-biblical, proclamatory tone; “That’s what … can do” is more conversational and slightly undercuts the grandiosity that makes the moment funnier in an atheist-modern context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The function is a mass command to start dancing; “Everybody dance!” is clear and energetic. It loses the slightly ‘announcer/MC’ flavor of the reference (which heightens the anachronistic Soviet-party vibe), but the comedic beat still lands.

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the self-satisfied opportunism of Miloslavsky arriving at exactly the right moment. “Perfect time to drop in” is natural and conveys the same smug luck; slightly less punchy than “good call,” but still effective.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
Imported caviar—eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key creative note: avoid calling eggplant spread “caviar” in English because it confuses viewers; the joke is the absurd elevation of a cheap spread. The actual translation keeps “caviar” for eggplant, directly violating that creative constraint and risking confusion. It also omits the comic beat of “Да!” and the ‘overseas’ framing as a separate flourish, reducing the build-up.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The humor is the escalating, status-item inventory and the repeated “three,” with the slightly bungled placement of “domestic” and the final delayed “three” for the jacket. The translation preserves the structure, the counts, and the comic rhythm well. Minor nuance: “movie cameras” is fine; “domestic” placement is acceptable. Overall effect intact.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Keeps the calm, sing-song fatalism and the shared ‘we’re all going to the psych ward’ punchline. Exclamation marks make it a bit more emphatic/less resigned than the reference’s ellipses, but it still plays as comic repetition.

summary.interpretation: 2.91 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.58 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
